problem_statement:
mktemp: fails with `--tmpdir=""`
When using `--tmpdir=""` (or `-p ""`) uutils `mktemp` fails whereas GNU `mktemp` doesn't.
```
cargo run -q mktemp -p ""
error: a value is required for '-p <DIR>' but none was supplied

For more information, try '--help'.
$ cargo run -q mktemp --tmpdir=""
error: a value is required for '--tmpdir[=<DIR>]' but none was supplied

For more information, try '--help'.
$ mktemp -p ""
/tmp/tmp.1jOFiMFSXf
$ mktemp --tmpdir=""
/tmp/tmp.qhIpkL93V0
```
GNU `mktemp` seems to handle an empty string as if no `DIR` has been provided: it uses `$TMPDIR` if set, else `/tmp`

It's the cause for https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/rust-coreutils/+bug/2117715
log_parser:
def parser(log: str) -> dict[str, str]:
    import re

    results: dict[str, str] = {}

    # Pattern for per-test lines from Rust libtest in verbose output.
    # Example: "test module::submodule::test_name ... ok"
    #          "test tests/by-util/test_more.rs - some doc (line) ... FAILED"
    test_line_re = re.compile(r'^\s*test\s+(.+?)\s+\.\.\.\s+(ok|FAILED|ignored)\b', re.IGNORECASE)

    # Track failures block listing
    in_failures = False

    for line in log.splitlines():
        # Match individual test lines
        m = test_line_re.match(line)
        if m:
            name, status = m.groups()
            status = status.lower()
            if status == "ok":
                results[name.strip()] = "pass"
            elif status == "failed":
                results[name.strip()] = "fail"
            elif status == "ignored":
                results[name.strip()] = "skip"
            continue

        # Detect failures block start
        if line.strip().lower().startswith("failures:"):
            in_failures = True
            continue

        if in_failures:
            s = line.strip()
            # End of failures block when we see the summary or another section
            if s.lower().startswith("test result:") or s.lower().startswith("error:"):
                in_failures = False
                continue
            # Collect failure names: lines that look like bare test names (non-empty)
            # Often like: "test_cat::test_write_fast_read_error"
            if s:
                # Avoid capturing headings like "---- ..." or other noise
                if not s.startswith("----") and not s.startswith("thread '"):
                    # In case there are trailing annotations, take the token up to first space
                    # But names here usually have no spaces; still be robust.
                    name = s.split()[0]
                    results[name] = "fail"

    return results
